question_structure.context:
A proton with mass $m_p$ and energy $E_p$ collides head-on with a photon of energy $E_b$. The collision results in the formation of a new particle with mass $m_\Delta$. This is a one-dimensional collision that conserves both relativistic energy and relativistic momentum. You may use the following constants where needed:
$g=9.8 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{kg}$
$k=1 / 4 \pi \epsilon_{0}=8.99 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{~N} \cdot \mathrm{m}^{2} / \mathrm{C}^{2}$
$c=3.00 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
$N_{\mathrm{A}}=6.02 \times 10^{23}(\mathrm{~mol})^{-1}$
$\sigma=5.67 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{~J} /\left(\mathrm{s} \cdot \mathrm{m}^{2} \cdot \mathrm{K}^{4}\right)$
$1 \mathrm{eV}=1.602 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{~J}$
$m_{e}=9.109 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{~kg}=0.511 \mathrm{MeV} / \mathrm{c}^{2}$
$m_{p}=1.673 \times 10^{-27} \mathrm{~kg}=938 \mathrm{MeV} / \mathrm{c}^{2}$
$\sin \theta \approx \theta-\frac{1}{6} \theta^{3}$ for $|\theta| \ll 1$
$G=6.67 \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{~N} \cdot \mathrm{m}^{2} / \mathrm{kg}^{2}$
$k_{\mathrm{m}}=\mu_{0} / 4 \pi=10^{-7} \mathrm{~T} \cdot \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{A}$
$k_{\mathrm{B}}=1.38 \times 10^{-23} \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{K}$
$R=N_{\mathrm{A}} k_{\mathrm{B}}=8.31 \mathrm{~J} /(\mathrm{mol} \cdot \mathrm{K})$
$e=1.602 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}$
$h=6.63 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{~J} \cdot \mathrm{s}=4.14 \times 10^{-15} \mathrm{eV} \cdot \mathrm{s}$
$(1+x)^{n} \approx 1+n x$ for $|x| \ll 1$
$\ln (1+x) \approx x$ for $|x| \ll 1$
$\cos \theta \approx 1-\frac{1}{2} \theta^{2}$ for $|\theta| \ll 1$
velocity parameter $\beta = \frac{v}{c}$
Lorentz factor $\gamma = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-\beta^{2}}}$
relativistic momentum $p = \gamma \beta m c$
relativistic energy $E = \gamma m c^{2}$
relativistic doppler shift $\frac{f}{f_{0}} = \sqrt{\frac{1-\beta}{1+\beta}}$
Relativistic particles obey the mass-energy relation: $E^{2}=(p c)^{2}+\left(m c^{2}\right)^{2}$
question_structure.sub_question_1: Determine $E_p$ in terms of $m_p$, $m_{\Delta}$, and $E_b$. You may assume that $E_b$ is small.
question_structure.sub_question_2: In this case, the photon energy $E_b$ is that of the cosmic background radiation, which is an EM wave with wavelength $1.06 \mathrm{~mm}$. Determine the energy of the photons, writing your answer in electron volts.
question_structure.sub_question_3: Assuming this value for $E_b$, what is the energy of the proton, in electron volts, that will allow the above reaction? This sets an upper limit on the energy of cosmic rays. The mass of the proton is given by $m_p c^{2}=938 \mathrm{MeV}$ and the mass of the $\Delta$ is given by $m_{\Delta} c^{2}=1232 \mathrm{MeV}$.

sub_question_1.step_1: We can solve the problem exactly first, approximating only in the last step. We set $c=1$ throughout, and transform to an inertial frame where the proton is initially at rest. Before the collision, $E_{p}=m_{p}$ and $E_{\gamma}=\left|p_{\gamma}\right|$.
sub_question_1.step_4: Combining these results, we have $\left(m_{p}+E_{\gamma}\right)^{2}=E_{\gamma}^{2}+m_{\Delta}^{2}$, which gives $E_{\gamma}=\frac{m_{\Delta}^{2}-m_{p}^{2}}{2 m_{p}}$.
sub_question_1.step_5: Now, transforming back to the original frame, we use the Doppler shift to relate the photon energies, $\alpha \equiv \frac{E_{b}}{E_{\gamma}}=\sqrt{\frac{1-\beta}{1+\beta}}$ where $\beta$ is the velocity parameter of the proton.
sub_question_1.step_6: Solving for $\beta$ in terms of $\alpha$, we get $\beta=\frac{1-\alpha^{2}}{1+\alpha^{2}}$.
sub_question_1.step_8: The proton energy in the original frame is $E_{p}=\gamma m_{p}=\frac{m_{p}}{2}\left(\alpha+\frac{1}{\alpha}\right)=\frac{m_{p}}{2}\left(\frac{2 m_{p} E_{b}}{m_{\Delta}^{2}-m_{p}^{2}}+\frac{m_{\Delta}^{2}-m_{p}^{2}}{2 m_{p} E_{b}}\right)$.
sub_question_1.step_9: Approximating since $E_b$ is small, we get  $E_{p} \approx \frac{m_{\Delta}^{2}-m_{p}^{2}}{4 E_{b}}$.
sub_question_1.step_10: Another method is to approximate throughout. In the lab frame, energy-momentum conservation gives $p_{p}-p_{b}=p_{\Delta}$ and $E_{p}+E_{b}=E_{\Delta}$.
sub_question_1.step_11: Squaring both expressions and dropping $E_b^2$ terms, we get $p_{p}^{2}-2 p_{p} p_{b} \approx p_{\Delta}^{2}$ and $E_{p}^{2}+2 E_{p} E_{b} \approx E_{\Delta}^{2}$.
sub_question_1.step_12: Subtracting these equations gives $m_{p}^{2}+2 E_{p} E_{b}+2 p_{p} E_{b}=m_{\Delta}^{2}$ which implies $E_{p}+p_{p}=\frac{m_{\Delta}^{2}-m_{p}^{2}}{2 E_{b}}$.
sub_question_1.step_13: Since $E_b$ is small, this quantity must be large. This means the protons are ultrarelativistic so $E_p \approx p_p$, giving $E_{p} \approx \frac{m_{\Delta}^{2}-m_{p}^{2}}{4 E_{b}}$.

sub_question_2.step_14: Using the given values and $E=\frac{h c}{\lambda}$, we can calculate the energy of the photons. $E = \frac{(6.63 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{J \cdot s})(3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{m/s})}{1.06 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{m}} = 1.879 \times 10^{-22} \mathrm{J}$. Converting this to electron volts, we get $1.879 \times 10^{-22} \mathrm{J} / (1.602 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{J/eV}) \approx 0.00117 \mathrm{eV}$ or $1.17 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{eV}$.

sub_question_3.step_15:
Using the result from part (a), we have $E_p \approx \frac{m_{\Delta}^{2}-m_{p}^{2}}{4 E_b}$. Using $m_pc^2=938 \mathrm{MeV}$ and $m_\Delta c^2=1232 \mathrm{MeV}$ and restoring the factors of $c$, we have 

$E_p = \frac{((1232 \mathrm{MeV})^2 - (938 \mathrm{MeV})^2)}{4 \times 1.17 \times 10^{-3}  \mathrm{eV} }$ 

$E_p = \frac{(1517824 - 880724) \mathrm{MeV}^2}{4 \times 1.17 \times 10^{-3}  \mathrm{eV} }$

$E_p = \frac{637100  \mathrm{MeV}^2}{4.68 \times 10^{-3}  \mathrm{eV} }$ $E_p =  \frac{637100  (10^6 \mathrm{eV})^2}{4.68 \times 10^{-3}  \mathrm{eV} }$ 

$E_p = 1.36  \times 10^{14}  \times 10^6 \mathrm{eV}$. Therefore, $E_{p} \approx 1.4 \times 10^{20} \mathrm{eV}$

step_3.physical_theorem: Conservation of Relativistic Energy and Momentum

step_4.physical_theorem: Algebraic manipulation of relativistic energy and momentum conservation equations
